AI.conclusion: Post-deposition annealing establishes uniaxial magnetic anisotropy in Ni thin films by enhancing magnetoelastic coupling at the film-substrate interface. The magnetic response follows the anisotropic strain imposed by lattice mismatch and CTE mismatch, demonstrating that strain engineering through annealing is an effective method to control magnetic anisotropy in ferromagnetic thin films.

title: 3D Imaging of Complex Skyrmion and Hopf Topologies in an Extended Sample
summary: Spin textures are key for emergent magnetic phenomena such as topological protection and underpin novel spintronic device paradigms based on racetrack memory, logic gates, and neuromorphic computing. Using a coherent diffractive imaging technique called vector ptycho-tomography, in combination with algorithms that are robust to noise, we image the 3D magnetic texture of skyrmion and Hopf topologies with no prior assumptions about the sample. This directly reveals experimentally for the first time an extended 3D skyrmion lattice, including the domain wall shape, topological charge, helicity, and Hopf index. Our findings demonstrate experimentally that dipole stabilized skyrmions in Fe/Gd multilayers exhibit barrel-shaped skyrmion tubes with a twisted helicity, transitioning from N$é$el-type winding at the surfaces to both clockwise and counterclockwise Bloch-type winding in the bulk, that can also be described as fractional hopfions. We image a lattice of 24 skyrmions with topological charge 1, average depth-dependent domain wall width of 23 to 40 nm, depth-dependent twisted helicity from $\pm$155$°$ to $\pm$30$°$, and fractional Hopf index of $\pm$0.3. Over 10 TB of data were analyzed to yield a fully-resolved 3D reconstruction over a >0.4 $μ$m$^3$ volume, with high fidelity down to the Nyquist limit of 8 nm. This method fills a key gap in the current landscape of magnetic imaging by enabling high-resolution, element-specific 3D reconstructions of full-field extended spin textures - offering a new route for exploring the topological complexity of magnetic materials in three dimensions.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first experimental visualization of 3D magnetic spin textures including extended skyrmion lattices and Hopf topologies using vector ptycho-tomography, a coherent diffractive imaging technique. The study reveals that dipole-stabilized skyrmions in Fe/Gd multilayers form barrel-shaped skyrmion tubes with twisted helicity that transitions from Néel-type at surfaces to Bloch-type in the bulk, which can be described as fractional hopfions.
AI.motivation: Magnetic spin textures are crucial for emergent magnetic phenomena like topological protection and underpin novel spintronic devices (racetrack memory, logic gates, neuromorphic computing). However, there was no method to directly image 3D magnetic textures without prior assumptions about the sample. This gap limited understanding of topological complexity in magnetic materials.
AI.method: The researchers used vector ptycho-tomography, a coherent diffractive imaging technique combined with noise-robust algorithms, to image 3D magnetic textures of skyrmion and Hopf topologies with no prior assumptions. They analyzed over 10 TB of data to achieve fully-resolved 3D reconstruction.
AI.result: They imaged a lattice of 24 skyrmions with topological charge 1, revealing barrel-shaped skyrmion tubes with twisted helicity. Key findings: average depth-dependent domain wall width of 23-40 nm, depth-dependent twisted helicity from ±155° to ±30°, and fractional Hopf index of ±0.3. The reconstruction achieved high fidelity down to 8 nm (Nyquist limit) over a >0.4 μm³ volume.
AI.conclusion: Vector ptycho-tomography fills a key gap in magnetic imaging by enabling high-resolution, element-specific 3D reconstructions of full-field extended spin textures. This method offers a new route for exploring topological complexity of magnetic materials in three dimensions, with potential applications in spintronic device development.
